summary: The Gemara explores the practical consequences of the Ben Zoma vs Rabbi Yehuda dispute about Temple immersion. Does failing to immerse invalidate Temple service? Does it violate a positive commandment? The leper analogy (tzaraat) is analyzed: a healed leper immerses the evening before - why? Multiple baraitot seem to support and contradict R. Yehuda's position. The page ends with Abaye's question to Rav Yosef about whether the Rabbis (who disagree with R. Yehuda) require two immersions in some cases.

glossary.en: A person afflicted with tzaraat (commonly mistranslated as leprosy); must undergo a purification process including immersion.
A positive commandment; violation of an aseh is less severe than a negative commandment.
Ezra's immersion decree; the requirement for those who experienced a seminal emission to immerse before Torah study or prayer.

rashiTranslations.en: Does failure to immerse before Temple service disqualify the avodah?
Ben Zoma: the service is disqualified. Baraita: without immersion, service is invalid.
R. Yehuda: failure to immerse violates a positive commandment but does not disqualify the service.
But R. Yehuda himself says lepers need not immerse the evening before - contradiction?
Resolution: lepers are different - they immerse the previous evening, not before service itself.
How does the objector reconcile R. Yehuda's view about lepers with his Temple immersion ruling?
Answer: lepers who immersed the previous evening are covered; but those who didn't need not immerse again specifically for morning service.
If distracted from avoiding corpse impurity - sprinkling is required, not immersion.
Both baraitot refer to non-distracted lepers - alternative resolution.
Ravina: the leper who immerses evening before is covered for morning service.
Abaye asks Rav Yosef: do the Rabbis require two immersions in some cases?
R. Yehuda: for a pure priest, one immersion suffices as a 'reminder'; the Rabbis require none beyond standard purity.
So too the leper analogy should hold.
But the leper must immerse even between services on his purification day.
The leper's immersions are explicitly written.
The KG's Yom Kippur immersions are also explicitly written.
Just as the KG's are written, so too they are needed.
Rav Pappa proposes a different derivation.
A kal vachomer from the leper - even less stringent cases require immersion.
Ben Zoma says: scriptural derivation.
R. Yehuda says: rabbinic safeguard.
Ben Zoma's scriptural derivation from the verse.
Ben Zoma derives from 'and bathe his body in water.'
R. Yehuda says: even without a verse there is rabbinic requirement.
As a precautionary safeguard.
What is the halachic difference between them?
The practical difference is whether the avodah performed without immersion is valid.
Ben Zoma: service without immersion is biblically invalid.
R. Yehuda: service is valid but a positive mitzvah was violated.
Ravina reconciles: Ben Zoma also holds the service is valid.
But a positive commandment was violated.
And R. Yehuda also agrees - the service is valid.
Abaye asks: is there a biblical source for R. Yehuda's position?
Question from Abaye: where is the biblical basis?
Perhaps the verse teaches only the mitzvah, not invalidation.
Ravina resolves: both agree service is valid post-facto.
Rashi commentary on line 37 of 30b.
Rashi commentary on line 38 of 30b.

Rashi commentary on line 48 of 30b.

display.title: Two consequences, one dispute: does skipping immersion invalidate the avodah?
display.whats: The practical stakes of the Ben Zoma vs R. Yehuda debate: does skipping the pre-service immersion disqualify the Temple service? The leper case tests R. Yehuda's position. Multiple baraitot are reconciled.
display.hint: Ben Zoma: biblical obligation = service disqualified if skipped. R. Yehuda: rabbinic precaution = service valid, but priest violated a positive commandment.

learning.learnerQuestion: If a priest skips the pre-service immersion, does that actually disqualify the Temple service, or is something less severe at stake?
learning.coreTension: The Ben Zoma/Rabbi Yehuda dispute from the previous daf is not merely theoretical: if the immersion is biblically mandated (Ben Zoma), skipping it should disqualify the Temple service entirely; if it is only a rabbinic precaution (Rabbi Yehuda), the service remains valid even though the priest has failed to fulfill a positive commandment. The Gemara then tests Rabbi Yehuda's position against a baraita about the healed leper (metzora), who immerses the evening before his purification day - a case that initially seems to require a further morning immersion under Rabbi Yehuda's logic, contradicting the baraita's silence on that point.
learning.coreMove: The Gemara resolves the apparent contradiction by treating the leper's case as distinct: the leper's evening immersion is its own established preparation, exempting him from immersing again the next morning. Ravina articulates this reconciliation. The sugya closes with Abaye putting a related question to Rav Yosef: do the Rabbis who disagree with Rabbi Yehuda actually require two separate immersions in certain edge cases?

takeaway.text: Whether an obligation is biblical or rabbinic is not just a label - it directly decides whether skipping it spoils the entire act (Ben Zoma) or only leaves a separate violation on the books while the service itself stands (Rabbi Yehuda).

learning.ahaMoment: The entire practical force of the dispute hinges on one question: does a purely procedural (non-purificatory) immersion, if skipped, affect the validity of Temple service? Ben Zoma says yes (it's biblical), R. Yehuda says no (it's procedural).
learning.learningBlocker: The sugya ends with Abaye's question to Rav Yosef about whether the Rabbis require two immersions in some cases, without recording an answer - and even Rabbi Yehuda's 'service remains valid' position does not address whether a priest who realizes mid-service that he skipped immersion should stop or continue.
learning.memoryAnchor: Ben Zoma = skip immersion, service invalid. R. Yehuda = skip immersion, service valid but you've violated a positive commandment.

argumentFlow.label: Does skipping immersion invalidate service?
argumentFlow.text: The practical question from the Ben Zoma/R. Yehuda dispute: if a priest skips the pre-service immersion, is the Temple service invalid?

argumentFlow.label: Ben Zoma: service disqualified
argumentFlow.text: According to Ben Zoma (biblical obligation), skipping the immersion should disqualify the Temple service.

argumentFlow.label: R. Yehuda: service valid, positive mitzva violated
argumentFlow.text: According to R. Yehuda (rabbinic precaution), the service remains valid but the priest has violated a positive commandment by failing to immerse.

argumentFlow.label: Leper baraita seems to contradict R. Yehuda
argumentFlow.text: A baraita about the healed leper's immersion the evening before service seems to contradict R. Yehuda's requirement for morning immersion.

argumentFlow.label: Leper's case is different
argumentFlow.text: The leper who immerses the evening before service is in a special category - the prior evening's immersion serves as preparation for the morning service.

argumentFlow.label: Ravina's reconciliation
argumentFlow.text: Ravina: the leper who immerses the evening before Yom Kippur service is exempt from the morning immersion because the evening immersion covers both.

argumentFlow.label: Abaye: do Rabbis require two immersions?
argumentFlow.text: Abaye asks Rav Yosef: in edge cases, do the Rabbis (who disagree with R. Yehuda) actually require two separate immersions?

narrative: series of baraita contradictions and resolutions

requiresUnderstanding: Ben Zoma vs R. Yehuda dispute from 30a

misconceptions.misconception: The practical consequence of the Ben Zoma/R. Yehuda dispute is minor.
misconceptions.correction: It determines whether skipping the immersion actually invalidates the Temple service, a highly significant question.

misconceptions.misconception: All opinions agree that missing the pre-service immersion disqualifies the avodah.
misconceptions.correction: R. Yehuda holds the service remains valid.

visualizableElements.label: Two outcomes of skipping pre-service immersion
visualizableElements.description: Ben Zoma: service invalid. R. Yehuda: service valid, but positive mitzva violated.

visualizableElements.label: Leper's immersion timing
visualizableElements.description: Evening before service: leper immerses. Morning of service: is another immersion required?

quizSeeds.question: According to Ben Zoma, what is the consequence if a priest enters Temple service without the pre-service immersion?
quizSeeds.answer: The Temple service is disqualified (pasul) - since the immersion is biblically mandated, skipping it invalidates the avodah.

quizSeeds.question: According to Rabbi Yehuda, what is the consequence if a priest skips the pre-service immersion?
quizSeeds.answer: The Temple service remains valid (not disqualified), but the priest has violated a positive commandment by failing to perform the required immersion.

sugyot.finalRuling: Disputed: Ben Zoma rules the service is invalid if immersion is skipped; R. Yehuda rules the service is valid but the priest has violated a positive commandment.
